violations.claim: Claim: For every `change` iterated, the symbols and labels maps contain an entry for `change.type`.
violations.smt2:
; LINE: 298
; PRINCIPLE: P2 – State Mutation / Alias Analysis
; Claim: For every `change` iterated, the symbols and labels maps contain an entry for `change.type`.
;   sym = symbols[change.type]; label = labels[change.type];
;   ⇒  change.type ∈ {"added","removed","regressed","fixed","unchanged"}.
; REACHABLE VIOLATION: a `change.type` outside the known keys would produce `sym = "?"` and `label = change.type`.
;   This violates the programmer’s intent that every type be mapped.

; Model a single change object:
(declare-const change_type String)

; The symbols/labels tables (explicitly enumerated by the source):
; we assert that the lookup returns “?” only when the key is unknown.
(define-fun sym ((t String)) String
  (ite (or (= t "added") (= t "removed") (= t "regressed") (= t "fixed") (= t "unchanged"))
       "known"
       "?"))

(define-fun label ((t String)) String
  (ite (or (= t "added") (= t "removed") (= t "regressed") (= t "fixed") (= t "unchanged"))
       "KNOWN"
       t))

; Assume the change type is *not* one of the known keys (bug scenario):
(assert (not (or (= change_type "added")
                 (= change_type "removed")
                 (= change_type "regressed")
                 (= change_type "fixed")
                 (= change_type "unchanged"))))

; The code would compute:
(declare-const symVal String)
(declare-const labelVal String)
(assert (= symVal (sym change_type)))
(assert (= labelVal (label change_type)))

; Violation condition: symVal = "?"  (means unmapped)  OR labelVal = change_type (raw, not a friendly label)
(assert (or (= symVal "?") (= labelVal change_type)))

(check-sat)   ; expected: sat

clause_history.clause:
; LINE: 298
; PRINCIPLE: P2 – State Mutation / Alias Analysis
; Claim: For every `change` iterated, the symbols and labels maps contain an entry for `change.type`.
;   sym = symbols[change.type]; label = labels[change.type];
;   ⇒  change.type ∈ {"added","removed","regressed","fixed","unchanged"}.
; REACHABLE VIOLATION: a `change.type` outside the known keys would produce `sym = "?"` and `label = change.type`.
;   This violates the programmer’s intent that every type be mapped.

; Model a single change object:
(declare-const change_type String)

; The symbols/labels tables (explicitly enumerated by the source):
; we assert that the lookup returns “?” only when the key is unknown.
(define-fun sym ((t String)) String
  (ite (or (= t "added") (= t "removed") (= t "regressed") (= t "fixed") (= t "unchanged"))
       "known"
       "?"))

(define-fun label ((t String)) String
  (ite (or (= t "added") (= t "removed") (= t "regressed") (= t "fixed") (= t "unchanged"))
       "KNOWN"
       t))

; Assume the change type is *not* one of the known keys (bug scenario):
(assert (not (or (= change_type "added")
                 (= change_type "removed")
                 (= change_type "regressed")
                 (= change_type "fixed")
                 (= change_type "unchanged"))))

; The code would compute:
(declare-const symVal String)
(declare-const labelVal String)
(assert (= symVal (sym change_type)))
(assert (= labelVal (label change_type)))

; Violation condition: symVal = "?"  (means unmapped)  OR labelVal = change_type (raw, not a friendly label)
(assert (or (= symVal "?") (= labelVal change_type)))

(check-sat)   ; expected: sat
